epigraph.text:
What has been will be again, what has been done will be 
done again; there is nothing new under the sun.

content.text:
If you add $3$ to a number, you can recover the original by 
subtracting $3$. If you multiply by $5$, you undo the operation by 
dividing by $5$. Every arithmetic operation has a natural 
*reverse*—and the same idea extends to functions. Given a function 
$f$ that transforms an input $x$ into an output $y$, can we find a function 
that starts with $y$ and recovers $x$? When such a reverse exists, we call 
it the **inverse** of $f$. Understanding inverses is essential 
preparation for calculus: in [ch:inverse-functions] we will need the inverse of 
the exponential function (the logarithm) and the inverses of the 
trigonometric functions, and in [ch:techniques-integration] these inverses will 
appear throughout our integration techniques.

Not every function can be reversed. Consider $f(x) = x^{2}$: both $3$ and 
$-3$ map to $9$, so if we know the output is $9$, we cannot determine 
whether the input was $3$ or $-3$. The function is "many-to-one," and a 
reversal would be "one-to-many"—which is not a function at all.

For an inverse to exist, the original function must never reuse an output 
value.

content.title: One-to-One (Injective) Function
content.content:
A function $f$ is **one-to-one** (or 
**injective**) if different inputs always produce different outputs:
\[
 x_1 \neq x_2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f(x_1) \neq f(x_2).
\]
Equivalently, $f(x_1) = f(x_2)$ implies $x_1 = x_2$.

content.title: The Horizontal Line Test
content.content:
A function is one-to-one if and only if no horizontal line intersects its 
graph more than once.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The logic mirrors the vertical line test from [sec:what-is-function]: 
a horizontal line at height $y = c$ collects all inputs that map to $c$. 
If the line hits the graph twice, two different inputs share the same 
output—so the function is not one-to-one.

content.title: Testing for one-to-one
content.problem:
Determine whether each function is one-to-one.

1. $f(x) = 3x - 7$

2. $g(x) = x^{3}$

3. $h(x) = x^{2} - 4$
content.solution:
1. Suppose $f(x_1) = f(x_2)$. Then $3x_1 - 7 = 3x_2 - 7$, giving 
 $x_1 = x_2$. **One-to-one.** (Every non-constant linear 
 function is one-to-one.)

2. Suppose $x_1^{3} = x_2^{3}$. Taking cube roots, $x_1 = x_2$. 
 **One-to-one.**

3. $h(2) = 0 = h(-2)$, yet $2 \neq -2$. **Not one-to-one.**

content.text:
George P\'{olya}{If you cannot solve the proposed problem, try 
to solve first some related problem. Could you solve a part of the problem?}

content.title: Inverse Function
content.content:
Let $f$ be a one-to-one function with domain $A$ and range $B$. The 
**inverse function** $f^{-1}$ is defined by
\[
 f^{-1}(y) = x \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad f(x) = y,
\]
for every $y \in B$. The domain of $f^{-1}$ is $B$ and its range is $A$.

content.text:
In other words, $f^{-1}$ "reverses" $f$:
\[
 f^{-1}\bigl(f(x)\bigr) = x \quad \text{for all } x \in A, \qquad
 f\bigl(f^{-1}(y)\bigr) = y \quad \text{for all } y \in B.
\]

content.title: The exponent $-1$ does not mean reciprocal
content.content:
The notation $f^{-1}(x)$ does **not** mean $\frac{1}{f(x)}$. The 
superscript $-1$ denotes the *inverse function*, not the reciprocal. 
To avoid confusion, we write the reciprocal as $\bigl[f(x)\bigr]^{-1}$ or 
$\frac{1}{f(x)}$.

content.title: Finding the Inverse of a One-to-One Function
content.content:
1. Write $y = f(x)$.

2. Swap $x$ and $y$: write $x = f(y)$.

3. Solve the resulting equation for $y$.

4. The solution is $y = f^{-1}(x)$.

content.title: Finding an inverse
content.solution:
1. Write $y = 2x + 5$.

2. Swap: $x = 2y + 5$.

3. Solve for $y$: $y = \dfrac{x - 5}{2}$.
Therefore $f^{-1}(x) = \dfrac{x-5}{2}$.

**Check:** $f(f^{-1}(x)) = 2\!\left(\dfrac{x-5}{2}\right) + 5 
= (x-5) + 5 = x.\;\checkmark$

content.title: Inverse of a rational function
content.problem: Find the inverse of $f(x) = \dfrac{3x - 1}{x + 2}$ and state its domain.
content.solution:
1. Write $y = \dfrac{3x - 1}{x + 2}$.

2. Swap: $x = \dfrac{3y - 1}{y + 2}$.

3. Solve for $y$:
 $$
 x(y+2) &= 3y - 1 \\
 xy + 2x &= 3y - 1 \\
 xy - 3y &= -1 - 2x \\
 y(x - 3) &= -2x - 1 \\
 y &= \frac{-2x - 1}{x - 3}.
 $$
Therefore $f^{-1}(x) = \dfrac{-2x - 1}{x - 3}$. The domain is all 
$x \neq 3$.

**Check:** The domain of $f$ is $x \neq -2$; the range of $f$ is 
$y \neq 3$ (the horizontal asymptote). This matches the domain of 
$f^{-1}$: $x \neq 3.\;\checkmark$

content.title: Restricting the domain to create an inverse
content.problem:
The function $f(x) = x^{2}$ is not one-to-one on $(-\infty, \infty)$. 
Find an inverse by restricting the domain to $[0, \infty)$.
content.solution:
On $[0, \infty)$, $f$ is one-to-one (it is increasing). Write $y = x^{2}$ 
with $x \geq 0$, swap to get $x = y^{2}$ with $y \geq 0$, and solve:
\[
 y = \sqrt{x}.
\]
Therefore $f^{-1}(x) = \sqrt{x}$ with domain $[0, \infty)$.

content.text:
If the point $(a, b)$ lies on the graph of $f$, then $f(a) = b$, so 
$f^{-1}(b) = a$, meaning $(b, a)$ lies on the graph of $f^{-1}$. 
Swapping coordinates $(a,b) \mapsto (b,a)$ is the geometric operation of 
**reflecting across the line $y = x$**.

content.title: Graph of the Inverse
content.content:
The graph of $f^{-1}$ is the reflection of the graph of $f$ across the 
line $y = x$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
Inverse functions will appear throughout calculus. In [ch:inverse-functions], 
the natural logarithm $\ln x$ is defined precisely as the inverse of 
$e^{x}$. In [ch:techniques-integration], we will encounter the inverse 
trigonometric functions $\arcsin x$, $\arccos x$, and $\arctan x$—created 
by restricting the domains of $\sin$, $\cos$, and $\tan$ so that they 
become one-to-one. And in [ch:derivative], we will learn a powerful 
formula for computing the derivative of *any* inverse function.

content.title: John Napier (1550--1617)
content.content:
The Scottish mathematician John Napier invented logarithms to simplify 
the enormous calculations required by astronomy and navigation. His 
*Mirifici Logarithmorum Canonis Descriptio* (1614) introduced the 
world to the idea that multiplication could be converted to addition by 
passing through an "inverse" relationship between exponential and 
logarithmic scales. A devout Presbyterian, Napier considered his 
theological writings to be his most important work, yet his mathematical 
invention transformed science. The astronomer Pierre-Simon Laplace later 
remarked that logarithms "doubled the life of astronomers" by halving 
the labor of their calculations.

content.caption: The horizontal line test determines whether a function is one-to-one.
content.alt: Two graphs. The left shows an increasing function that passes the horizontal line test - each horizontal line crosses it at most once. The right shows a parabola that fails - horizontal lines cross it twice.

content.caption: A function and its inverse are reflections across the line $y = x$.
content.alt: A coordinate plane showing a function curve and its inverse. The dashed line y=x is the axis of symmetry. Each point (a,b) on the original function corresponds to point (b,a) on the inverse.

marginNotes.text:
By their fruit you will recognize them. Do 
people pick grapes from thornbushes, or figs from thistles?

In their hearts humans plan their course, 
but the Lord establishes their steps.

marginNotes.author: Sir Isaac Newton
marginNotes.text:
If I have seen further, it is by standing on 
the shoulders of giants.
